section.title: Building the Tabernacle
section.sectionRationale: What God commanded in chapters 25 and 26 is now actually built. The people's generosity so overflows that Moses must restrain further giving, and Bezalel, Oholiab, and every skilled craftsman construct the tabernacle's curtains, coverings, frames, and veil exactly as the pattern required.
section.thesis: The skilled craftsmen begin the work with more than enough materials already given, and they construct the tabernacle's inner curtains, outer coverings, wooden frames, and dividing veil precisely as the LORD had commanded Moses.
section.frameNote: Five movements: the craftsmen begin work and the people's giving must be restrained; the ten inner curtains; the goat hair tent covering; the upright frames and crossbars; and the veil separating the two chambers.

units.label: More than enough
units.note: the people were restrained from bringing more

media.alt: Craftsmen surrounded by piles of gold and cloth
media.caption: The people bring so much that Moses must command them to stop giving.

verses.text: “So Bezalel, Oholiab, and every {{skilled|skilled}} person are to carry out everything commanded by the LORD, who has given them skill and ability to know how to perform all the work of constructing the sanctuary.”
Then Moses summoned Bezalel, Oholiab, and every skilled person whom the LORD had gifted, everyone whose heart stirred him to come and do the work.

They received from Moses all the contributions that the Israelites had brought to carry out the service of constructing the sanctuary. Meanwhile, the people continued to bring freewill offerings morning after morning,
so that all the skilled craftsmen who were doing all the work on the sanctuary left their work
and said to Moses, “The people are bringing more than enough for doing the work the LORD has commanded us to do.”

After Moses had given an order, they sent a proclamation throughout the camp: “No man or woman should make anything else as an offering for the sanctuary.” So the people were restrained from bringing more,
since what they already had was more than enough to perform all the work.

All the skilled craftsmen among the workmen made the ten curtains for the tabernacle. They were made of finely spun linen, as well as blue, purple, and scarlet yarn, with cherubim skillfully worked into them.
Each curtain was twenty-eight cubits long and four cubits wide; all the curtains were the same size.
And he joined five of the curtains together, and the other five he joined as well.
He made loops of blue material on the edge of the end curtain in the first set, and also on the end curtain in the second set.
He made fifty loops on one curtain and fifty loops on the end curtain of the second set, so that the loops lined up opposite one another.
He also made fifty gold clasps to join the curtains together, so that the tabernacle was a unit.

units.label: The outer coverings
units.note: goat hair, ram skins, fine leather

verses.text: He then made curtains of goat hair for the tent over the tabernacle, eleven curtains in all.
Each of the eleven curtains was the same size, thirty cubits long and four cubits wide.
He joined five of the curtains into one set and the other six into another.
He made fifty loops along the edge of the end curtain in the first set, and fifty loops along the edge of the corresponding curtain in the second set.
He also made fifty bronze clasps to join the tent together as a unit.
Additionally, he made for the tent a covering of ram skins dyed red, and over that a covering of fine leather.

units.label: The frames and crossbars
units.note: as the LORD had commanded Moses

verses.text: Next, he constructed upright frames of acacia wood for the tabernacle.
Each frame was ten cubits long and a cubit and a half wide.
Two tenons were connected to each other for each frame. He made all the frames of the tabernacle in this way.
He constructed twenty frames for the south side of the tabernacle,
with forty silver bases to put under the twenty frames, two bases for each frame, one under each tenon.
For the second side of the tabernacle, the north side, he made twenty frames
and forty silver bases, two bases under each frame.
He made six frames for the rear of the tabernacle, the west side,
and two frames for the two back corners of the tabernacle,
coupled together from bottom to top and fitted into a single ring. He made both corners in this way.
So there were eight frames and sixteen silver bases, two under each frame.
He also made five crossbars of acacia wood for the frames on one side of the tabernacle,
five for those on the other side, and five for those on the rear side of the tabernacle, to the west.
He made the central crossbar to run through the center of the frames, from one end to the other.
And he overlaid the frames with gold and made gold rings to hold the crossbars. He also overlaid the crossbars with gold.

units.label: The veil and the entrance curtain

verses.text: Next, he made the veil of blue, purple, and scarlet yarn, and finely spun linen, with cherubim skillfully worked into it.
He also made four posts of acacia wood for it and overlaid them with gold, along with gold hooks; and he cast four silver bases for the posts.
For the entrance to the tent, he made a curtain embroidered with blue, purple, and scarlet yarn, and finely spun linen,
together with five posts and their hooks. He overlaid the tops of the posts and their bands with gold, and their five bases were bronze.

skilled.gloss: Skilled or wise of heart, literally wise-hearted. This phrase links craftsmanship directly to wisdom, treating fine practical skill not as a lesser ability but as a genuine expression of God-given wisdom applied to physical work.

basic.context: This chapter records the actual construction beginning: so much material had already been given that Moses had to stop the people from bringing more. Bezalel, Oholiab, and the skilled craftsmen then build the tabernacle's inner curtains, outer coverings, wooden frames, and dividing veil exactly according to the pattern given back in chapters 25 and 26.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a straightforward historical record of construction matching earlier divine instruction almost word for word. Its significance lies less in hidden symbolism within each repeated detail and more in the striking fact of obedience: what God commanded is now, at last, actually done, faithfully and abundantly.
basic.originalLanguages: The craftsmen are repeatedly called chakham lev, wise or skilled of heart, a phrase that treats fine practical craftsmanship as a genuine form of wisdom, not a lesser, merely manual skill. The narrative's near word for word repetition of the earlier instructions underscores exact obedience to the pattern shown Moses.
basic.typology: The overflowing abundance that must be restrained anticipates the New Testament's vision of a church so generous in giving that need itself becomes rare, Acts 4:34. The precise correspondence between command and construction shows a pattern of faithful obedience that anticipates Christ's own perfect obedience to the Father's will.
basic.christ: Just as the tabernacle was built exactly as commanded, Jesus consistently declares that He does exactly what the Father commands Him, John 5:19, 8:29, the perfect fulfillment of faithful, obedient construction of God's purposes. The overflowing generosity here also anticipates the church's Spirit-filled generosity in Acts, itself a fruit of union with Christ.
basic.god: God's detailed instructions are met here with equally detailed obedience, showing that He cares as much about faithful execution as about the initial vision. His provision through the people's hearts is so abundant that restraint, not scarcity, becomes the problem, revealing the sufficiency of what He supplies for His own purposes.
basic.crossReferences: Acts 4:32-35 describes the early church's similar overflowing generosity, so that there was no needy person among them. John 5:19 and 8:29 show Jesus doing exactly what the Father commands, the perfect fulfillment of obedient construction. 1 Corinthians 3:10-15 speaks of building carefully on the foundation God has laid.
basic.oneStory: God's detailed pattern for His dwelling place is met with detailed, faithful obedience and such overflowing generosity that giving must be restrained. This pattern of abundant provision and exact obedience to God's design points forward to Christ's own perfect obedience to the Father and the church's Spirit-given generosity in building up God's true dwelling place.

context.literary: This chapter closely mirrors chapters 25 and 26 nearly verse for verse, moving from command to execution, a literary technique common in ancient Near Eastern building accounts to emphasize precise, faithful fulfillment of divine instruction.
context.historical: The detail that giving had to be restrained is a striking, almost unique moment in the Old Testament of a fundraising effort producing surplus rather than shortfall, reflecting genuine, widespread repentance and devotion after the golden calf.
context.canonical: This pattern of command followed by exact fulfillment recurs throughout Scripture's building narratives, including the construction of Solomon's temple and, ultimately, the New Testament's vision of the church as a spiritual house built according to God's design, 1 Peter 2:5.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record the tabernacle's actual construction as a faithful, detailed fulfillment of God's earlier instructions, showing both the abundance of the people's response and the craftsmen's precise obedience to the pattern given.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This is a historical narrative describing the specific construction of Israel's tabernacle; it does not prescribe building methods for churches today. Its abiding truth is the value of faithful, careful obedience to God's revealed will and the blessing of a generosity so full it must be restrained.

debates.question: Why does the text repeat the tabernacle's specifications almost word for word from chapters 25 and 26 rather than simply summarizing the construction?

views.label: Emphasis on precise, faithful obedience
views.case: Many interpreters see the deliberate repetition as underscoring that Israel built exactly what God commanded, without deviation, a literary technique reinforcing the theological point that faithful obedience matters down to the details, not only in broad intention.

views.label: Ancient literary convention for building accounts
views.case: Others note that ancient Near Eastern texts commonly repeated command and execution nearly verbatim as a standard literary convention for royal and temple building projects, so the repetition may reflect genre convention as much as deliberate theological emphasis.

debates.bearing: Both explanations agree that the repetition, whichever its primary cause, functions within the text to highlight exact, faithful correspondence between what God commanded and what Israel actually built.

study.originalLanguages: The workmen are called chakham lev, wise or skilled of heart, treating fine craftsmanship as a genuine expression of wisdom rather than mere manual labor. The chapter's near exact repetition of the earlier commanded specifications in Hebrew reinforces, at the level of the text itself, that construction matched instruction precisely.

typology.type: The overflowing generosity that had to be restrained
typology.fulfillment: The early church's similarly abundant generosity, so that there was no needy person among them, Acts 4:32-35.

typology.type: Exact construction according to the commanded pattern
typology.fulfillment: Christ's perfect obedience to the Father's will in all things, John 5:19, 8:29.

study.christ: Jesus repeatedly says He does exactly what the Father shows and commands Him, John 5:19, doing nothing on His own initiative, the ultimate fulfillment of the pattern seen here: faithful construction according to a given design, without deviation. The overflowing generosity of Israel's response also anticipates the fruit of the Spirit poured out on the early church, whose members held everything in common so fully that need itself nearly disappeared, Acts 4:34.
study.god: God's people respond to His detailed instructions with equally detailed obedience and such overflowing generosity that abundance, not scarcity, becomes the concern, showing that when hearts are truly stirred toward God, His purposes are provided for richly. God's care for precision in His dwelling place reflects His care that His purposes be carried out faithfully, not merely approximately.

crossReferences.note: The early church's overflowing generosity parallels Israel's abundant giving here.
Jesus does exactly what the Father shows Him, the perfect fulfillment of obedient construction.
Believers are built into a spiritual house, echoing the language of careful, purposeful building.

study.oneStory: Israel's overflowing generosity and the craftsmen's exact obedience to God's pattern turn command into a completed dwelling place for God among His people. That same pattern, abundant provision met with faithful obedience, is fulfilled perfectly in Christ, who always does exactly what the Father commands, and continued in the church, built up as God's true spiritual house.

quiz.q: Why did Moses eventually have to order the people to stop bringing offerings?

options.text: Because they had already brought more than enough to complete all the work.
options.note: Verses 5 to 7 explicitly state this reason.

options.text: Because the offerings were of poor quality.
options.note: The problem was abundance, not quality; the materials given were more than sufficient.

options.text: Because a famine required the materials be used for food instead.
options.note: No such famine or repurposing is mentioned; the issue was simply surplus for the building project.

options.text: The people never brought enough and construction was delayed.
options.note: Verses 5 through 7 explicitly describe abundance requiring restraint, not a shortage.

quiz.q: What was worked into both the ten inner curtains and the veil?

options.text: Cherubim, skillfully worked into the blue, purple, and scarlet yarn.
options.note: Verses 8 and 35 both describe cherubim worked into these two textiles.

options.text: Images of the sun and moon.
options.note: The described imagery is cherubim, not celestial bodies.

options.text: The names of the twelve tribes.
options.note: Tribal names appear on the priestly garments' stones, not woven into these curtains and the veil.

options.text: No imagery was included.
options.note: Verses 8 and 35 explicitly describe cherubim worked into both textiles.

quiz.q: What material were the tabernacle's outer coverings made from, layered over the goat hair tent?

options.text: Ram skins dyed red, and over that fine leather.
options.note: Verse 19 describes this exact layered covering.

options.text: Solid sheets of bronze.
options.note: Bronze was used for clasps, bases, and utensils, not as an outer tent covering.

options.text: A single layer of undyed cotton.
options.note: The coverings were specifically ram skins dyed red and fine leather, not undyed cotton.

options.text: No additional covering was made.
options.note: Verse 19 explicitly describes these layered coverings.

quiz.q: How were the tabernacle's acacia wood frames finished before assembly?

options.text: Overlaid with gold, with gold rings made to hold the crossbars.
options.note: Verse 34 describes this exact finishing of the frames.

options.text: Left as bare, unfinished wood.
options.note: The frames were specifically overlaid with gold, not left unfinished.

options.text: Painted with blue dye to match the curtains.
options.note: The frames were overlaid with gold, not painted with dye.

options.text: No finishing process is described.
options.note: Verse 34 explicitly describes the gold overlay and rings.

quiz.q: What material made up the bases for the veil's four posts?

options.text: Silver.
options.note: Verse 36 specifies four silver bases for the veil's posts.

options.text: Bronze.
options.note: Bronze bases were used for the entrance curtain's posts, verse 38, while the veil's posts specifically stood on silver bases.

options.text: Solid gold.
options.note: Gold overlaid the posts themselves and their hooks, but the bases underneath were silver, not solid gold.

options.text: No bases are mentioned for the veil's posts.
options.note: Verse 36 explicitly specifies four silver bases.

questions: The people gave so generously that Moses had to tell them to stop. What would it look like for your own giving, of time, money, or skill, to become this kind of overflowing rather than measured and cautious?
The craftsmen are called wise-hearted, treating their practical skill as true wisdom offered to God. Where do you undervalue practical, hands-on skill as a genuine form of worship and service to God?

The construction matched God's earlier instructions almost exactly, without deviation. Where in your own life are you tempted to improvise on God's revealed will rather than following it faithfully in the details?

Cherubim were woven into both the innermost curtains and the veil, a consistent reminder of guarded holiness throughout the structure. Where do you need a renewed sense of reverence in how you approach God, even in the ordinary parts of your life?

This chapter records patient, careful, detailed work, not dramatic events. How do you view the slow, unglamorous, faithful tasks in your own life, as lesser than the dramatic moments of faith, or as genuine acts of worship in their own right?

translationNotes.note: More than enough is a rare note of abundance in an ancient building account, where shortages of materials or labor were the more common concern; it reflects the depth of Israel's post-golden-calf generosity.

This section closely repeats the specifications given in Exodus 26:1-37 nearly verbatim, a literary pattern common in ancient Near Eastern building narratives to emphasize exact correspondence between divine command and human execution.

The veil described here corresponds exactly to the instructions of Exodus 26:31, underscoring that the same barrier separating the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place, later torn at Christ's death, was built precisely according to God's specified design.
